skill.name: remotion-render
skill.description: Render videos from React/Remotion component code via inference.sh. Pass TSX code, get MP4. Supports all Remotion APIs: useCurrentFrame, useVideoConfig, spring, interpolate, AbsoluteFill, Sequence. Configurable resolution, FPS, duration, codec. Use for: programmatic video generation, animated graphics, motion design, data-driven videos, React animations to video. Triggers: remotion, render video from code, tsx to video, react video, programmatic video, remotion render, code to video, animated video, motion graphics code, react animation video
skill.summary: Render videos from React/Remotion TSX code to MP4 via inference.sh API
skill.author: tul-sh

security_audit.summary: This is a documentation skill that provides usage examples for the inference.sh video rendering service. All static findings are false positives: external_commands patterns are bash examples in documentation, network patterns are legitimate service URLs, and weak cryptographic algorithm flags are misidentified YAML/table content. No actual security risks present.

medium_findings.title: External Command Examples in Documentation
medium_findings.description: The skill contains bash command examples using infsh CLI. These are documentation showing how to use the service, not actual code execution. No user input is passed to shell commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: All bash code blocks are examples showing CLI usage. The skill only provides documentation, not executable code that processes user input.

medium_findings.title: External Service URLs Referenced
medium_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs to inference.sh and remotion.dev are legitimate service endpoints, not data exfiltration.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: These URLs are the official documentation and API endpoints for the video rendering service being documented.

content.value_statement: Transform React/Remotion TSX components into MP4 videos programmatically. Pass code, get video output with full control over resolution, FPS, duration, and animations.
actual_capabilities: Render React/Remotion TSX code to MP4 video via inference.sh API
Support all Remotion APIs: useCurrentFrame, useVideoConfig, spring, interpolate, Sequence
Configure video output: resolution, FPS, duration, codec
Pass dynamic props to components for data-driven videos
Create animations: fade-ins, counters, spring physics, sequences
limitations: Requires inference.sh account and CLI installation
Video rendering happens on remote servers, not locally
Complex animations may require longer render times
Limited to codecs supported by inference.sh service

use_cases.title: Generate Animated Social Media Content
use_cases.description: Create branded video content for social media using code. Perfect for automated marketing campaigns.
use_cases.target_user: Marketing teams and social media managers

use_cases.title: Build Data-Driven Video Presentations
use_cases.description: Generate videos that visualize data from APIs or databases. Use props to inject dynamic content.
use_cases.target_user: Data analysts and developers building automated reports

use_cases.title: Create Video Prototypes for Motion Design
use_cases.description: Quickly prototype video concepts and animations using React code before final production.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Video Render
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the remotion-render skill to render a video with a simple 'Hello World' text fade-in animation over 3 seconds at 30fps.

prompt_templates.title: Animated Counter Video
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a video that shows an animated percentage counter from 0% to 100% over 5 seconds using Remotion. Render at 60fps with 1080x1080 resolution.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Sequence Animation
prompt_templates.prompt: Render a video with three sequential text elements that fade in one after another. Use Sequence component from Remotion. Duration: 4 seconds at 30fps.

prompt_templates.title: Custom Props Video
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a video using custom props for title and subtitle. Pass title='Product Launch' and subtitle='Coming Soon' to create a promotional announcement video.

output_examples.input: Render a video with a spring animation of a white square on purple background
output_examples.output: Video file downloaded to local directory. The video shows a white square with rounded corners that scales using spring physics on a purple (#6366f1) background.

output_examples.input: Create a welcome video with fade-in text animation
output_examples.output: MP4 video file rendered with 'Welcome' text that fades in over 2 seconds on a dark blue (#1a1a2e) background.

best_practices: Start with simple animations and incrementally add complexity
Use appropriate FPS based on animation smoothness needs (30fps for simple, 60fps for smooth motion)
Test with shorter durations first to verify render behavior before longer outputs
anti_patterns: Do not include external image or video assets that require network fetching during render
Avoid overly complex component trees that can cause render timeouts
Do not assume local file system access within the render environment

faq.question: Do I need to install anything to use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes, you need the inference.sh CLI (infsh). Install it via: npx skills add inference-sh/skills@agent-tools

faq.question: Can I use external images in my video?
faq.answer: No, the render environment does not have network access. Include all assets as base64 or use the Img component with pre-hosted URLs.

faq.question: What video formats are supported?
faq.answer: The service supports common formats including MP4. Specify codec in the input parameters if needed.

faq.question: How long does rendering take?
faq.answer: Render time depends on duration, resolution, and complexity. Simple 3-second videos typically take 30-60 seconds.

faq.question: Can I pass dynamic data to my video?
faq.answer: Yes, use the props parameter to pass dynamic values like titles, numbers, or data from APIs.

faq.question: Is this free to use?
faq.answer: The skill is free to use but requires an inference.sh account. Check their pricing for render costs.
